name: Hornachos and Oppidum Romano — circular route through the Sierra de los Honachos
name_original: Hornachos y Oppidum Romano — circular por la Sierra de los Honachos
region_intro: Touring cycling around Hornachos, located in Extremadura, Spain, offers diverse landscapes for exploration. The region is characterized by the Sierra Grande de Hornachos, a mountain range with rocky outcrops and Mediterranean forests. Extensive dehesa landscapes, featuring holm oaks and cork oaks, provide serene routes. Waterways like the Palomillas and Matachel rivers also traverse the area, contributing to its natural beauty. The terrain transitions from open plains to rolling hills, offering varied inclines and descents. Best touring cycling routes around Hornachos The most popular touring cycling route is Hornachos and Oppidum Romano — circular route through the Sierra de los Honachos, a 19.9 miles (32.0 km) trail that takes 2 hours 17 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores the varied terrain of the Sierra de los Honachos. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Atrio del Cristo loop from Ribera del Fresno, a moderate 15.9 miles (25.5 km) path. This route offers views of the surrounding dehesa landscapes and takes approximately 2 hours 14 minutes. Local touring cyclists also love the Pavilion Shelter Near Palomas loop from Puebla de la Reina, a 23.1 miles (37.2 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 5 minutes. Touring cycling around Hornachos is defined by the Sierra Grande de Hornachos, dehesa landscapes, and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, with all routes being of moderate difficulty. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 8 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Hornachos's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when touring cycling around Hornachos?
A: Hornachos offers a diverse landscape for touring cyclists. You'll encounter a mix of open plains, rolling hills, and mountainous areas, particularly within the Sierra Grande de Hornachos. The routes often traverse extensive &lt;em&gt;dehesa&lt;/em&gt; landscapes with holm oaks and cork oaks, as well as follow river valleys like the Palomillas and Matachel. This variety provides both gentle stretches and more challenging inclines.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes available in Hornachos?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes around Hornachos are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. For example, the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022024213/hornachos-and-oppidum-romano-circular-route-through-the-sierra-de-los-honachos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hornachos and Oppidum Romano — circular route through the Sierra de los Honachos&lt;/a&gt; is a great option for exploring the varied terrain.

Q: What natural features will I encounter on touring cycling routes in Hornachos?
A: Cyclists will experience the rich natural beauty of the region. Routes often pass through the Sierra Grande de Hornachos, a designated Zone of Special Protection for Birds (ZEPA), offering rocky outcrops and Mediterranean forests. You'll also cycle through picturesque &lt;em&gt;dehesa&lt;/em&gt; landscapes, characterized by holm and cork oaks, and alongside waterways such as the Matachel River. Keep an eye out for diverse flora and fauna.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks to see while cycling in Hornachos?
A: Absolutely. While cycling, you can explore historical sites like the remains of the 9th-century Hornachos Castle, which offers panoramic views. The region is also home to prehistoric cave paintings in rock shelters like Menina and Salto de la Moza, which can be visited with guided tours. Additionally, you might pass by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5332673" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Hinojosa del Valle&lt;/a&gt; or the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5336471" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Matachel River Bridge&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I combine touring cycling with birdwatching in Hornachos?
A: Yes, Hornachos is an excellent destination for combining touring cycling with birdwatching. The Sierra Grande de Hornachos is a Zone of Special Protection for Birds (ZEPA), making it a haven for various species. Cyclists often have the opportunity to spot significant birds such as the Spanish imperial eagle, tawny vulture, golden eagle, and eagle owl while enjoying the scenic routes.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Hornachos?
A: The spring and autumn months are generally ideal for touring cycling in Hornachos. During these seasons, the weather is typically mild and pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter. This allows for comfortable rides and optimal enjoyment of the natural landscapes.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling routes in Hornachos?
A: While all routes listed are of moderate difficulty, the varied terrain means some sections might be more suitable for families with older children or those with some cycling experience. Routes that stick to the flatter &lt;em&gt;dehesa&lt;/em&gt; areas or river banks might be more comfortable. Always check the route details, including distance and elevation, to ensure it matches your family's ability.

Q: Is parking available near the touring cycling routes in Hornachos?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in and around Hornachos, particularly in the town center and near popular starting points for trails. For specific routes, it's advisable to check local information or maps for designated parking areas to ensure convenient access to your chosen cycling path.

Q: What is the typical elevation gain on touring cycling routes in Hornachos?
A: The elevation gain on touring cycling routes in Hornachos varies depending on the specific path, reflecting the region's mix of plains and hills. For instance, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14656335" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hornachos loop from Hornachos&lt;/a&gt; can have an elevation gain of over 400 meters, while others like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40355590" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Atrio del Cristo loop from Ribera del Fresno&lt;/a&gt; feature around 200 meters of ascent. All routes are categorized as moderate difficulty.

Q: Are there any facilities or shelters along the touring cycling routes?
A: Some routes may feature facilities or shelters. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21931737" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pavilion Shelter Near Palomas loop from Puebla de la Reina&lt;/a&gt; passes by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7554604" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pavilion Shelter Near Palomas&lt;/a&gt;. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, as facilities might be sparse on more remote sections of the trails.

Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Hornachos?
A: There are at least 5 dedicated touring cycling routes around Hornachos featured in this guide, all offering a moderate challenge. These routes showcase the best of the region's diverse landscapes, from the Sierra Grande to the dehesa and river valleys.

Q: What makes Hornachos a unique destination for touring cyclists?
A: Hornachos stands out due to its unique combination of the Sierra Grande, a 'green island' amidst plains, and the characteristic &lt;em&gt;dehesa&lt;/em&gt; landscapes. It's a designated ZEPA, offering exceptional birdwatching opportunities, and boasts historical sites like Hornachos Castle and prehistoric cave paintings. This blend of natural beauty, ecological importance, and cultural heritage provides a rich and varied cycling experience.
tips: The town of Hornachos rises on the same mountain range that carries the snow up to the highest point, the Iglesia de la Purísima Concepción. From this point you can see the entire population and landscape in the background and above, the castle that bears the same name as the nucleus, on the massif.
Adventure along gravel roads along old cattle tracks, you will reach the ruins of a fortified Roman settlement dating from the 2nd century, situated on a small hill in the Matachel Valley.
